Here’s a detailed explanation of the YouTube video you shared and what it’s about, based on its content and associated information I could find:

📹 Video Title: College Management System || School Management System || LazyCoder
🔗 Source: YouTube link you provided (https://youtu.be/XIYdVPKQpiQ) 

🧠 What the Video Is About
This video is a project demonstration/tutorial showing a College Management System / School Management System — basically an educational institute software project. 

Here’s what it likely covers:

👉 Overview of a management system used for handling students, staff, attendance, tests, and results. 

👉 It’s part of a project playlist by LazyCoder, a creator/channel that makes programming project tutorials on YouTube. 

👉 Typically, these types of videos show:

Features of the system

Basic workflows (take attendance, assign results, view reports)

The interface of the management system

Sometimes even basic source code explanation or demo

Because it’s part of a series like School Management System and College Management System, the video serves as a demo/guide for building such a project — especially helpful for students doing software projects or programmers learning how to make educational software. 

🛠️ What Was Used to Create It (Likely)
Although the video doesn’t have a full technical description visible without watching it, based on the channel’s usual content and typical practice for such projects:

✔ Common Technologies Used
These kinds of systems are usually built with:

Programming Language:

Could be Python (Django/Flask), PHP (Laravel), JavaScript (Node.js) or C#/Java — depending on the tutorial series.

Many “management system” tutorials use Python Django because it’s popular for database-driven applications. 

Database:

A relational database like MySQL, SQLite, or PostgreSQL is typical for storing:

Student records

Attendance data

Test scores

Frontend:

Basic HTML/CSS templates

JavaScript for interactions (optional)

Software Tools:

A code editor/IDE (like VS Code, PyCharm)

A local server/hosting environment to run and demo the app

🧩 Typical Features in Such Projects
These systems generally include:

User roles: Admin, teacher, student

Attendance tracking and viewing reports

Exam/Test record entry & display

Student management (CRUD operations)

These features are common in educational project tutorials for students who need a complete software solution example — especially in college courses or coding classes. 

📌 Summary (Simple)
✔ It's a project demo/tutorial video about a College/School Management System.
✔ The creator/channel is LazyCoder, which posts software project guides. 

✔ The system shown manages students, attendance, tests, results — typical features for educational software. 

✔ Technologies used are likely common web or software development stacks such as Python Django or similar frameworks with a database backend.

  i got this in terminal

Good ✅ This error is very common — and very easy to fix.

It simply means:

👉 Django is not installed
OR
👉 Your virtual environment is not activated

Nothing scary. We fix it step-by-step.

🔍 STEP 1 — Check Python First
In your terminal run:

python --version
If it shows Python version → good ✅
If not → Python is not installed properly.

🔥 STEP 2 — Create Virtual Environment (Very Important)
Go inside your project folder first:

cd C:\Users\lenovo
Now create venv:

python -m venv venv
🚀 STEP 3 — Activate Virtual Environment
In PowerShell run:

venv\Scripts\activate
If it works, you will see:

(venv) PS C:\Users\lenovo>
If you get execution policy error, run this once:

Set-ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Scope Process
Then activate again.

📦 STEP 4 — Install Django
After activation:

pip install django
Wait until installation completes.

🔎 STEP 5 — Check Django
Now run:

python -m django --version
If it shows version number → Perfect ✅

🏗 STEP 6 — Now Create Project
Instead of django-admin, use this safer command:

python -m django startproject config .
This always works.

💡 Why This Error Happened?
Because:

django-admin command works only if Django is installed globally

But we are using virtual environment (which is correct way)

So we use:

python -m django
🎯 Do This Now
Follow steps 1 → 6 exactly.

Then send me:

Screenshot or output of terminal

Tell me if (venv) appears

Once it works, we move to:

🔐 Phase 2: Creating Apps & Custom User Model (Professional Level Setup)

You created another virtual environment inside college_erp,
but you did NOT install Django inside that new venv.

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'
means:

👉 This new venv is empty
👉 Django is not installed in it

🔥 Let’s Fix Cleanly (Step-By-Step)
You are currently here:

C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp

✅ STEP 2 — Run Migrate
Now run:

python manage.py migrate
It should work now.

🧠 Why This Happened?
You earlier had:

C:\Users\lenovo\venv
Then you created:

C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp\venv
Two different environments.

Each venv is isolated.

So packages installed in one are NOT available in another.

That’s why Django disappeared.

⚠️ IMPORTANT DECISION
Now we must choose ONE clean structure:

Option A (Recommended)
Keep venv inside project:

college_erp/
    venv/
    manage.py
    college_erp/

🚀 PHASE 4 — Professional ERP Structure Setup

🧠 Now We Build Like Real ERP
Next big step:

🔐 Custom User Model (VERY IMPORTANT)
Why?

Because ERP has:

Admin

Faculty

Student

And Django default user is not enough for ERP.

So we create role-based user system.

This is what makes your project PROFESSIONAL level.

🏗 STEP 1 — Configure Custom User Model
Open:

accounts/models.py
Replace everything with this:

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser
from django.db import models


class CustomUser(AbstractUser):

    USER_TYPE_CHOICES = (
        ('admin', 'Admin'),
        ('faculty', 'Faculty'),
        ('student', 'Student'),
    )

    user_type = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=USER_TYPE_CHOICES)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.username
Save it.

🧠 STEP 2 — Tell Django to Use Custom User
Open:

college_erp/settings.py
Add this at bottom:

AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.CustomUser'
🧠 STEP 3 — Add accounts App First in INSTALLED_APPS
Make sure in settings.py:

'accounts',
is ABOVE other custom apps.

Example:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'accounts',
    'students',
    'faculty',
    'academics',
    'finance',
    'communication',
Very important.

🚨 IMPORTANT
Since we changed user model,
we must RESET database.

Delete:

db.sqlite3
Then delete all migrations folders inside each app
(except __init__.py).

🔥 Then Run:
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
If no errors → PERFECT.

🎯 Why We Are Doing This?
Because in viva when examiner asks:

How did you manage multiple user roles?

You will say:

I implemented a custom AbstractUser model with role-based access control.

Instant impression 💎🔥

🚀 After This Reply:
Custom user model configured successfully

Then we move to:

😂😂 Relaxxx Prateek…

Tumne db.sqlite3 ko command ki tarah run kar diya.

PowerShell soch raha hai tum koi program run karna chahte ho.

🧠 Important
db.sqlite3 is a FILE
Not a command.

Isliye error aaya.

✅ What You Actually Needed To Do
If database reset karna ho, you:

❌ Do NOT type:
db.sqlite3
✅ Instead delete it like this:
del db.sqlite3
OR manually:

Open folder

Right click → Delete

From your output:

👉 Migrations already ran successfully

Error:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'academics'
This means:

👉 Django is trying to load 'academics' from INSTALLED_APPS
👉 But that app does NOT exist in your current project folder

This happened because you:

Created new project again

Structure got mixed

Some apps are in different folder level

No problem. We fix cleanly now.

🔎 STEP 1 — Check Your Current Folder Structure
Inside:

C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp

core
accounts
students
faculty
academics
finance
communication
manage.py
venv
OR maybe only:

college_erp
manage.py
venv
We need to confirm.

🧠 Why This Error Happens
In settings.py you probably have:

'academics',
But there is no folder named academics in same level as manage.py.

Django only loads apps that exist in same directory as manage.py.

🚀 QUICK FIX (Most Likely Solution)
If you don't actually see academics folder:

👉 Open college_erp/settings.py

Find:

INSTALLED_APPS
Remove these if they don't exist:

'academics',
'finance',
'communication',
'core',
Keep only the apps that physically exist.

Save.

Then run:

python manage.py migrate

After you deleted db.sqlite3,
you must run:

python manage.py migrate
BEFORE creating superuser.

Run dir

Perfect 👍 now I understand clearly.

Your error:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'academics'
This means only ONE thing:

👉 'academics' is written inside INSTALLED_APPS
👉 But Django cannot find that app at project level

This is happening because your structure is like this:

college_erp/
    college_erp/   ← project folder
    core/
    accounts/
    students/
    faculty/
    academics/
    finance/
    communication/
    manage.py
    venv/
OR

You accidentally created apps inside a nested folder like:

college_erp/
    college_erp/
        academics/
Django only detects apps that are at SAME LEVEL as manage.py.

🔥 Let’s Fix This Cleanly
STEP 1 — Check Exact Structure Properly
Run this command:

dir /ad
This will show folder names properly.

Send me that output.

💡 Most Likely Fix (99% Case)
Open:

college_erp/settings.py
Find:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
Now temporarily change it to ONLY this:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
]
Remove everything else.

Save.

Now run:

python manage.py migrate
Then:

python manage.py createsuperuser
This will work 100%.

Multiple project creations

Nested project folder

Apps possibly inside wrong folder

Good 👍 you're using PowerShell, not CMD — that’s why dir /ad failed.

In PowerShell, use this instead:

Get-ChildItem -Directory
or short version:

ls
From your output I can already see:

You are inside:

C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp
And there are 8 folders + manage.py

That’s correct structure 👍

Now the real question is:

👉 Is academics directly inside this folder
OR
👉 Inside the inner college_erp folder?

🔎 Run This Command
ls academics
If it shows files like:

__init__.py
admin.py
apps.py
models.py
views.py
migrations/
Then academics is correct.

🔎 Also Run This
ls college_erp
If you see academics inside THAT folder, then that is the problem.

⚠️ 99% Likely Issue
Your structure might be:

college_erp/
    college_erp/
        academics/   ❌ WRONG PLACE
Apps must be here:

college_erp/
    academics/       ✅ CORRECT
    manage.py

'academics',

python manage.py migrate

Good 👍 now we finally see the REAL problem.

This line tells everything:

ls academics
Cannot find path 'C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp\academics'
👉 That means academics folder does NOT exist at all

But inside settings.py you still have:

'academics',
So Django is trying to load an app that does not exist — that’s why:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'academics'
🎯 Why This Happened
You probably:

Deleted the academics folder

Or created it in a different project

Or deleted db.sqlite3 and cleaned something incorrectly

Right now your project has no academics app, but settings still references it.

✅ FIX (Very Simple)
Option 1 — Remove It (Fastest Fix)
Open:

college_erp/settings.py
Find:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
Remove this line:

'academics',
Also remove any other app that does not physically exist in this folder.

Save.

Now run:

python manage.py migrate
python manage.py createsuperuser
This will work immediately.

✅ Option 2 — Recreate the App (If You Need It)
If you actually want academics app:

Run:

python manage.py startapp academics
Then run:

python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
Done.

🚨 IMPORTANT LESSON
Django rule:

👉 If app name is in INSTALLED_APPS
👉 That folder MUST physically exist beside manage.py

Otherwise Django crashes.

Just fix project and continue?

🧱 STEP 1 — Create accounts App (Always First)
In Django projects, custom user model must be created first before any migrations for other apps.

Run:

python manage.py startapp accounts
Check structure:

ls
You should now see:

accounts/
config/
manage.py
venv/
🧠 STEP 2 — Create Custom User Model
Open:

accounts/models.py
Replace everything with:

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser
from django.db import models

class CustomUser(AbstractUser):
    USER_TYPE_CHOICES = (
        ('ADMIN', 'Admin'),
        ('STAFF', 'Staff'),
        ('STUDENT', 'Student'),
    )

    user_type = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=USER_TYPE_CHOICES)
Save.

⚙️ STEP 3 — Register App
Open:

config/settings.py
Add inside INSTALLED_APPS:

'accounts',
So it looks like:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',

    'accounts',
]
⚠️ STEP 4 — Tell Django to Use Custom User
In settings.py, at bottom add:

AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.CustomUser'
VERY IMPORTANT: This must be done BEFORE running migrate again.

🗑 STEP 5 — Reset Database (Because we changed user model)
Delete database:

del db.sqlite3
Delete migrations inside accounts (if any created automatically):

Go to:

accounts/migrations/
Delete everything inside except __init__.py

🏗 STEP 6 — Migrate Fresh
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
👤 STEP 7 — Create Superuser
python manage.py createsuperuser
Run server:

python manage.py runserver
Login admin panel.

students/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from django.conf import settings

class Student(models.Model):
    user = models.OneToOneField(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    enrollment_number = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    course = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    year = models.IntegerField()

    def __str__(self):
        return self.user.username
🛠 Now Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate

Open:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Student

admin.site.register(Student)

Students
inside admin panel.

🏫 STEP 3 — Create Faculty App
Stop server (CTRL + C)

Run:

Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'faculty',
🧠 Create Faculty Model (Linked to CustomUser)
Open:

faculty/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from django.conf import settings

class Faculty(models.Model):
    user = models.OneToOneField(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    department = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    designation = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    salary = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.user.username
🛠 Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
🔐 Register in Admin
Open:

faculty/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Faculty

admin.site.register(Faculty)
Run server again:

python manage.py runserver
Check /admin

You should now see:

Students
Faculties
🧠 Important Concept
Notice what we are doing:

We are not creating separate login tables.

We are using ONE CustomUser table and linking:

Student → OneToOne → CustomUser

Faculty → OneToOne → CustomUser

This is how real ERP systems are designed.

Confirm once Faculty appears in admin.

Then we build:

📚 Courses

📚 STEP 4 — Create Courses App
Stop server.

Run:

python manage.py startapp courses
Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'courses',
🧠 Create Course Model (Linked to Faculty)
Open:

courses/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from faculty.models import Faculty

class Course(models.Model):
    course_name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    course_code = models.CharField(max_length=20, unique=True)
    faculty = models.ForeignKey(Faculty, on_delete=models.SET_NULL, null=True)
    credits = models.IntegerField()

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.course_name} ({self.course_code})"
🛠 Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
🔐 Register in Admin
Open:

courses/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Course

admin.site.register(Course)
Run server:

python manage.py runserver
Go to /admin

You should now see:

Courses
🧠 What We Just Built
Relationship structure now:

CustomUser
   ├── Student (OneToOne)
   ├── Faculty (OneToOne)

Faculty
   └── Course (ForeignKey)
This is real relational database design 👏

🎯 Next Step
After you confirm Courses appear, we will build:

📅 Attendance system
→ Link Student + Course
→ Add Date + Status

That’s where ERP starts becoming powerful.

Current database design:

CustomUser
   ├── Student (OneToOne)
   └── Faculty (OneToOne)

Faculty
   └── Course (ForeignKey)

📅 STEP 5 — Attendance System
This is where real ERP logic begins.

Attendance must connect:

Student

Course

Date

Status (Present/Absent)

🧱 Create Attendance App
Stop server.

Run:

python manage.py startapp attendance
Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'attendance',
🧠 Create Attendance Model
Open:

attendance/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from students.models import Student
from courses.models import Course

class Attendance(models.Model):
    STATUS_CHOICES = (
        ('PRESENT', 'Present'),
        ('ABSENT', 'Absent'),
    )

    student = models.ForeignKey(Student, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    course = models.ForeignKey(Course, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    date = models.DateField()
    status = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=STATUS_CHOICES)

    class Meta:
        unique_together = ('student', 'course', 'date')

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.student} - {self.course} - {self.date}"
🛠 Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
🔐 Register in Admin
Open:

attendance/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Attendance

admin.site.register(Attendance)
Run server again:

python manage.py runserver
Go to /admin

You should now see:

Attendance

🎯 What We Will Build

🧱 Create Exams App
Stop server.

Run:

python manage.py startapp exams
Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'exams',
🧠 Create Exam & Result Models
Open:

exams/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from courses.models import Course
from students.models import Student

class Exam(models.Model):
    EXAM_TYPE_CHOICES = (
        ('MIDTERM', 'Mid Term'),
        ('FINAL', 'Final'),
        ('QUIZ', 'Quiz'),
    )

    course = models.ForeignKey(Course, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    exam_type = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=EXAM_TYPE_CHOICES)
    date = models.DateField()
    total_marks = models.IntegerField()

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.course} - {self.exam_type}"


class Result(models.Model):
    student = models.ForeignKey(Student, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    exam = models.ForeignKey(Exam, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    marks_obtained = models.IntegerField()

    class Meta:
        unique_together = ('student', 'exam')

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.student} - {self.exam} - {self.marks_obtained}"
🛠 Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
🔐 Register in Admin
Open:

exams/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Exam, Result

admin.site.register(Exam)
admin.site.register(Result)
Run server:

python manage.py runserver
Go to /admin

You should now see:

Exams
Results

Student
   └── Fee
🧱 Create Finance App
Stop server.

Run:

python manage.py startapp finance
Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'finance',
🧠 Create Fee Model
Open:

finance/models.py
Replace with:

from django.db import models
from students.models import Student

class Fee(models.Model):
    STATUS_CHOICES = (
        ('PAID', 'Paid'),
        ('PARTIAL', 'Partial'),
        ('UNPAID', 'Unpaid'),
    )

    student = models.ForeignKey(Student, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    total_amount = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)
    amount_paid = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, default=0)
    payment_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)
    status = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=STATUS_CHOICES)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.student} - {self.status}"

    @property
    def due_amount(self):
        return self.total_amount - self.amount_paid
🛠 Run Migrations
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
🔐 Register in Admin
Open:

finance/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Fee

admin.site.register(Fee)
Run server:

python manage.py runserver
Go to /admin

You should now see:

Fees

🔐 STEP 8 — Role-Based Dashboard (Admin / Faculty / Student)
Right now everyone logs into /admin.

That’s not how real ERP works.

We will build:

👨‍🎓 Student dashboard

👨‍🏫 Faculty dashboard

👑 Admin dashboard

Automatic redirect based on user_type

Remember your CustomUser has:

user_type = models.CharField(...)
We will now USE it properly.

🧱 STEP 1 — Create Core App (For Dashboard + Home)
Stop server.

Run:

python manage.py startapp core
Add to INSTALLED_APPS:

'core',
🧠 STEP 2 — Create Dashboard Views
Open:

core/views.py
Replace with:

from django.shortcuts import render, redirect
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

@login_required
def dashboard(request):
    user = request.user

    if user.user_type == 'ADMIN':
        return render(request, 'core/admin_dashboard.html')

    elif user.user_type == 'STAFF':
        return render(request, 'core/faculty_dashboard.html')

    elif user.user_type == 'STUDENT':
        return render(request, 'core/student_dashboard.html')

    return redirect('login')
🌐 STEP 3 — Create URLs
Create file:

core/urls.py
Add:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('dashboard/', views.dashboard, name='dashboard'),
]
🔗 STEP 4 — Connect to Main URLs
Open:

config/urls.py
Update it to:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('core.urls')),
]
🗂 STEP 5 — Create Templates
Create folder:

core/templates/core/
Inside create:

admin_dashboard.html
<h1>Admin Dashboard</h1>
faculty_dashboard.html
<h1>Faculty Dashboard</h1>
student_dashboard.html
<h1>Student Dashboard</h1>
⚙️ STEP 6 — Configure Login Redirect
In settings.py, add at bottom:

LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/dashboard/'
LOGOUT_REDIRECT_URL = '/admin/login/'
🛠 STEP 7 — Assign User Types
Go to /admin

Edit your users:

Set:

Admin user → ADMIN

Create one faculty user → STAFF

Create one student user → STUDENT

🚀 TEST IT
Run:

python manage.py runserver
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
Login.

You should be redirected to different dashboards based on role.

Not Found: /
[15/Feb/2026 12:14:37] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 404 2418

Perfect — this is a small configuration mistake, not a big problem.

Error:

NoReverseMatch: Reverse for 'login' not found.
This happens because we wrote:

return redirect('login')
But we NEVER created a login URL.

Django does not automatically create login unless we include its auth URLs.

✅ FIX (2 Steps)
🔹 STEP 1 — Add Django Auth URLs
Open:

config/urls.py
Change it to this:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),

    # Django built-in login/logout
    path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),

    path('', include('core.urls')),
]
This enables:

/accounts/login/
/accounts/logout/
And registers the name login.

🔹 STEP 2 — Fix Redirect in Dashboard View
Open:

core/views.py
Change this:

return redirect('login')
To this:

return redirect('/accounts/login/')
OR better:

from django.urls import reverse
return redirect(reverse('login'))
✅ OPTIONAL (Cleaner Redirect)
In settings.py add:

LOGIN_URL = '/accounts/login/'
Now @login_required will automatically redirect there.

🚀 Now Test Again
Restart server:

python manage.py runserver
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
It should:

Redirect to login if not logged in

Redirect to correct dashboard if logged in

🧠 Why / Is Showing 404?
Because we didn’t create a homepage route.

That’s normal.

You should access:

/dashboard/
/accounts/login/
/admin/
Tell me what happens after this fix.

[15/Feb/2026 12:17:07] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 404 2570

✅ Everything is working correctly.

The message:

Not Found: /
GET / HTTP/1.1 404
is completely normal.

It just means:

👉 You don’t have a route for / (homepage).
👉 Django only knows about:

/admin/

/accounts/login/

/dashboard/

So when you visit:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
There is no view for /, so Django shows 404.

🎯 What You Should Visit Instead
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
OR

http://127.0.0.1:8000/accounts/login/
OR

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
🚀 OPTIONAL (Make / Open Dashboard Automatically)
If you want / to open dashboard, do this:

Open:

core/urls.py
Change it to:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.dashboard, name='home'),
    path('dashboard/', views.dashboard, name='dashboard'),
]
Now / will work.

Restart server.

Now visiting:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
will show dashboard.

🧠 Important
Your system is NOT broken.
This is normal Django routing behavior.

Now tell me:

When you visit /dashboard/ and login as:

ADMIN → does it show Admin Dashboard?

STUDENT → Student Dashboard?

STAFF → Faculty Dashboard?

If yes → your role-based system is working correctly 🔥

Perfect ✅ — now we’ve found the real issue.

Your error is:

TemplateDoesNotExist: registration/login.html
This is NOT a URL problem anymore.

This means:

👉 Django’s built-in auth system is working
👉 But it cannot find the login template file

🎯 Why This Happens
When you use:

path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls'))
Django automatically looks for this template:

templates/registration/login.html
You have not created it yet.

So Django crashes.

✅ FIX (Very Easy)
🔹 Step 1 — Create Templates Folder
Inside your project root (same level as manage.py) create:

college_erp/
    templates/
        registration/
So the structure becomes:

college_erp/
│
├── core/
├── config/
├── templates/
│    └── registration/
│         └── login.html   ← create this
└── manage.py
🔹 Step 2 — Create login.html
Inside:

templates/registration/login.html
Paste this:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Login</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Login Page</h2>

    <form method="post">
        {% csrf_token %}
        {{ form.as_p }}
        <button type="submit">Login</button>
    </form>

</body>
</html>
Save it.

🔹 Step 3 — Ensure Templates Setting Is Correct
Open:

config/settings.py
Find TEMPLATES and make sure this exists:

'DIRS': [BASE_DIR / 'templates'],
It should look like:

TEMPLATES = [
    {
        'BACKEND': 'django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates',
        'DIRS': [BASE_DIR / 'templates'],
        'APP_DIRS': True,
        ...
    },
]
🚀 Now Restart Server
python manage.py runserver
Now visit:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
It should:

Redirect to /accounts/login/

Show login form

Allow login

Redirect to dashboard

🧠 Important
Your admin panel is working.
Your models are working.
Your URLs are working.

The only missing piece was:

login.html
You are very close to having a fully functional ERP authentication system 🔥

👉 Does login page appear now?

[15/Feb/2026 12:25:55] "GET /dashboard/ HTTP/1.1" 302 0

Good 👍 — now everything is working structurally.

The new issue is this pattern:

GET /dashboard/ → 302
GET /accounts/login/ → 200
POST /accounts/login/ → 302
GET /dashboard/ → 302
GET /accounts/login/ → 200
That means:

👉 You login
👉 It redirects to /dashboard/
👉 /dashboard/ sends you BACK to login
👉 Loop 🔁

This is called a login redirect loop.

🎯 Why This Happens
Your dashboard view probably looks like this:

@login_required
def dashboard(request):
    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return render(request, 'admin_dashboard.html')

    elif request.user.role == 'student':
        return render(request, 'student_dashboard.html')

    elif request.user.role == 'faculty':
        return render(request, 'faculty_dashboard.html')

    else:
        return redirect('login')
The problem is this line:

return redirect('login')
If the logged-in user does not match any condition,
you send them back to login — even though they are already logged in.

So Django keeps bouncing.

✅ FIX IT PROPERLY
🔹 Step 1 — Remove redirect('login')
Change your dashboard view to this:

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from django.http import HttpResponse

@login_required
def dashboard(request):

    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return HttpResponse("Admin Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'student':
        return HttpResponse("Student Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'faculty':
        return HttpResponse("Faculty Dashboard")

    else:
        return HttpResponse("Logged in, but no role assigned.")
⚠️ IMPORTANT:
Do NOT redirect to login inside dashboard.

@login_required already handles that.

🔹 Step 2 — Add This To settings.py
LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/dashboard/'
LOGIN_URL = '/accounts/login/'
This tells Django:

After login → go to dashboard

🔥 What Is Happening Right Now?
Most likely:

You are logging in with a normal user
But that user has:

no role field
OR

role not matching your conditions

So your code sends them back to login.

🧠 Very Important Question
Are you using:

Default Django User model?
OR

Custom User model with role field?

If you are using default User, then:

request.user.role
WILL NOT EXIST.

That causes logic issues.

🚀 Quick Test
After updating dashboard view:

Restart server

Login again

Tell me exactly what text appears

You should now see:

Admin Dashboard
OR

Student Dashboard
OR

Faculty Dashboard
We are very close to finishing your role-based login system 💪


Page not found (404)
Request Method:	GET
Request URL:	http://127.0.0.1:8000/
Using the URLconf defined in config.urls, Django tried these URL patterns, in this order:

admin/
accounts/
dashboard/ [name='dashboard']
The empty path didn’t match any of these.

You’re seeing this error because you have DEBUG = True in your Django settings file. Change that to False, and Django will display a standard 404 page.

Perfect ✅ — this is completely normal and your project is actually working correctly.

Django is telling you:

The empty path didn’t match any of these.
Your available URLs are:

/admin/

/accounts/

/dashboard/

But you are visiting:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
That is the empty path (""), and you did NOT create a route for it.

So Django shows 404. ✔️ That is expected behavior.

✅ Two Ways To Fix This
🔹 Option 1 (Recommended) — Make / Open Dashboard
Open:

core/urls.py
Make it look like this:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.dashboard, name='home'),   # ← add this
    path('dashboard/', views.dashboard, name='dashboard'),
]
Restart server.

Now visiting:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
will open the dashboard.

🔹 Option 2 — Just Use /dashboard/
Instead of /, simply visit:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
This already works.

Perfect 👌 Let’s move forward properly.

Since you're building a College ERP, the correct next step is:

🎓 Step 1: Create Student Management System
This is the foundation of any ERP.

✅ Step A — Create Student Model
Open:

core/models.py
Add this:

from django.db import models

class Student(models.Model):
    first_name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    last_name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    roll_number = models.CharField(max_length=20, unique=True)
    department = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    year = models.IntegerField()

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.first_name} {self.last_name}"
Save.

✅ Step B — Make Migrations
Run:

python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
You should see:

Applying core.000X...
OK
✅ Step C — Register Student in Admin
Open:

core/admin.py
Add:

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Student

admin.site.register(Student)
Save.

✅ Step D — Test in Admin Panel
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
Login.

You should now see:

Students
Click → Add Student → Save.

If this works, your database + model layer is correct 🎉

🚀 What You Just Built
You now have:

A database table for students

Admin interface for managing students

Unique email & roll number validation

Proper Django model structure

This is real backend development now.

🎯 Step 1 — Create Student List View
Open:

core/views.py
Add:

from django.shortcuts import render
from .models import Student

def student_list(request):
    students = Student.objects.all()
    return render(request, "core/student_list.html", {"students": students})
Save.

🎯 Step 2 — Create URL
Open:

core/urls.py
Add:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path("students/", views.student_list, name="student_list"),
]
Make sure this app is included in config/urls.py:

path("", include("core.urls")),
🎯 Step 3 — Create Template
Create folders:

core/templates/core/
Inside that create:

student_list.html
Paste this:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Students</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Student List</h1>

    <table border="1">
        <tr>
            <th>Name</th>
            <th>Email</th>
            <th>Roll Number</th>
            <th>Department</th>
            <th>Year</th>
        </tr>

        {% for student in students %}
        <tr>
            <td>{{ student.first_name }} {{ student.last_name }}</td>
            <td>{{ student.email }}</td>
            <td>{{ student.roll_number }}</td>
            <td>{{ student.department }}</td>
            <td>{{ student.year }}</td>
        </tr>
        {% endfor %}
    </table>

</body>
</html>
Save.

🚀 Full CRUD for Students (Create, Update, Delete)
Right now you can only view students.
Now we’ll allow:

➕ Add student

✏ Edit student

❌ Delete student

This makes your ERP practical.

🎯 STEP 1 — Create Student Form
Open:

core/forms.py
If it doesn't exist, create it.

Add:

from django import forms
from .models import Student

class StudentForm(forms.ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        model = Student
        fields = "__all__"
Save.

🎯 STEP 2 — Update Views
Open:

core/views.py
Add these imports:

from django.shortcuts import render, redirect, get_object_or_404
from .forms import StudentForm
Now add these new views:

def add_student(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm()
    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


def edit_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST, instance=student)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm(instance=student)
    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


def delete_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)
    student.delete()
    return redirect("student_list")
Save.

🎯 STEP 3 — Add URLs
Open:

core/urls.py
Update it:

urlpatterns = [
    path("students/", views.student_list, name="student_list"),
    path("students/add/", views.add_student, name="add_student"),
    path("students/edit/<int:pk>/", views.edit_student, name="edit_student"),
    path("students/delete/<int:pk>/", views.delete_student, name="delete_student"),
]
Save.

🎯 STEP 4 — Create Form Template
Create:

core/templates/core/student_form.html
Paste:

{% extends "core/base.html" %}

{% block content %}
<h2>Student Form</h2>

<form method="POST">
    {% csrf_token %}
    {{ form.as_p }}
    <button type="submit">Save</button>
</form>

{% endblock %}
Save.

🎯 STEP 5 — Add Buttons to Student List
Open:

student_list.html
Modify table:

Add new column:

<th>Actions</th>
Inside loop add:

<td>
    <a href="{% url 'edit_student' student.pk %}">Edit</a> |
    <a href="{% url 'delete_student' student.pk %}">Delete</a>
</td>
Also above table add:

<a href="{% url 'add_student' %}">Add Student</a>

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from django.http import HttpResponse

@login_required
def dashboard(request):

    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return HttpResponse("Admin Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'student':
        return HttpResponse("Student Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'faculty':
        return HttpResponse("Faculty Dashboard")

    else:
        return HttpResponse("Logged in, but no role assigned.")


from django.shortcuts import render
from .models import Student

def student_list(request):
    students = Student.objects.all()
    return render(request, "core/student_list.html", {"students": students})

from django.shortcuts import render, redirect, get_object_or_404
from .forms import StudentForm

def add_student(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm()
    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


def edit_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST, instance=student)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm(instance=student)
    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


def delete_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)
    student.delete()
    return redirect("student_list")  my view.py looks like it

Very good 👌 your views.py is mostly correct.

But there are two improvements we should make to make your ERP more professional and secure.

✅ 1️⃣ First Issue — Duplicate Imports (Not Wrong, Just Clean It)
Right now you imported render twice:

from django.shortcuts import render
from django.shortcuts import render, redirect, get_object_or_404
Not an error, but messy.

✅ Clean & Proper Version of views.py
Replace your entire file with this clean version:

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from django.http import HttpResponse
from django.shortcuts import render, redirect, get_object_or_404

from .models import Student
from .forms import StudentForm


@login_required
def dashboard(request):

    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return HttpResponse("Admin Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'student':
        return HttpResponse("Student Dashboard")

    elif hasattr(request.user, 'role') and request.user.role == 'faculty':
        return HttpResponse("Faculty Dashboard")

    else:
        return HttpResponse("Logged in, but no role assigned.")


@login_required
def student_list(request):
    students = Student.objects.all()
    return render(request, "core/student_list.html", {"students": students})


@login_required
def add_student(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm()
    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


@login_required
def edit_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)

    if request.method == "POST":
        form = StudentForm(request.POST, instance=student)
        if form.is_valid():
            form.save()
            return redirect("student_list")
    else:
        form = StudentForm(instance=student)

    return render(request, "core/student_form.html", {"form": form})


@login_required
def delete_student(request, pk):
    student = get_object_or_404(Student, pk=pk)
    student.delete()
    return redirect("student_list")
🔥 What We Improved
✅ Clean imports
✅ Added @login_required to all student views
→ Now only logged-in users can manage students

That is how a real ERP should behave.

⚠ Important Question
Do you already have a custom role field in your User model?

Because this part:

hasattr(request.user, 'role')
Will ONLY work if:

You created a custom user model
OR

You extended User with a profile model

If you did NOT create that yet, this condition will always fail.

🎯 Now Test
Log out

Try visiting:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/students/
It should redirect to login page.

If yes → security working ✅

Now tell me:

Do you want to properly implement role-based system next?
That’s the next big professional step.

Perfect ✅ everything is working properly.

There is no error in your logs.

Let me explain what’s happening clearly 👇

✅ 1️⃣ Server Reload Messages
This:

core\views.py changed, reloading.
Is normal.

Django auto-reloads whenever you edit a file.

That is GOOD.

✅ 2️⃣ Migrations Applied Successfully
Applying core.0001_initial... OK
Means:

✔ Student table created in database
✔ Database synced

✅ 3️⃣ /students/ is Working
You see:

GET /students/ HTTP/1.1" 200
200 = Success

So your CRUD + template + login protection is working.

⚠ 4️⃣ The Only “Issue”
This line:

Not Found: /
GET / HTTP/1.1" 404
Means:

You don’t have a homepage route /

That is normal.

Because your urls only have:

/admin/

/students/

/dashboard/

There is no:

path("", something)
So Django shows 404.

🔥 Easy Fix (Professional Way)
Open:

core/urls.py
Add this at top:

path("", views.dashboard, name="home"),
Now your file should look like:

urlpatterns = [
    path("", views.dashboard, name="home"),
    path("students/", views.student_list, name="student_list"),
    path("students/add/", views.add_student, name="add_student"),
    path("students/edit/<int:pk>/", views.edit_student, name="edit_student"),
    path("students/delete/<int:pk>/", views.delete_student, name="delete_student"),
]
Save → refresh /

Now / will open dashboard instead of 404.

Right now your dashboard checks:

hasattr(request.user, 'role')
But unless you created a custom user model, this will NOT properly work.

So now we build:

🚀 Proper Role-Based Authentication System
We will create:

👤 Admin

🎓 Student

👨‍🏫 Faculty

Using Django Groups (professional + simple method)

🎯 WHY We Use Groups (Best Practice)
Instead of creating a custom user model (complex), we use:

Django Groups

Built-in
Secure
Industry standard
Perfect for college ERP

🛠 STEP 1 — Create Groups in Admin
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
Click:

Authentication & Authorization → Groups

Create 2 groups:

Student

Faculty

Save.

🛠 STEP 2 — Assign Users to Groups
Go to:

Users → Select a user

Scroll to Groups → Add:

Add user to Student group
OR

Add user to Faculty group

Save.

🛠 STEP 3 — Update Dashboard Logic (IMPORTANT)
Now open:

core/views.py
Replace your dashboard function with this:

@login_required
def dashboard(request):

    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return HttpResponse("Admin Dashboard")

    elif request.user.groups.filter(name='Student').exists():
        return HttpResponse("Student Dashboard")

    elif request.user.groups.filter(name='Faculty').exists():
        return HttpResponse("Faculty Dashboard")

    else:
        return HttpResponse("Logged in but no role assigned.")
Save.

🧪 Test It
Create one user → add to Student group

Create another user → add to Faculty group

Login with each user

Visit:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
You should see different dashboards.

🎯 Why This Is Powerful
Now your ERP:

Has authentication

Has role separation

Is secure

Is structured

Follows real-world architecture

This is how professional systems work.

🎯 STEP 2 — Assign Users to Groups (Detailed Guide)

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/

Authentication and Authorization

👉 Users

👉 Add user

Fill:

Username → student1

Password → anything

Confirm password

Click Save.

🔹 Step 2.4 — Assign Group to User (IMPORTANT)
After saving, you’ll see full user edit page.

Scroll down to:

👉 Groups

On right side you will see:

Available groups
Select:

Student

Then click arrow → to move it to:

Chosen groups
Click Save.

Create another user:

Username → faculty1

Assign group → Faculty

Save.

User	Group

🧠 How It Works Internally
This line checks group:

request.user.groups.filter(name='Student').exists()
Django checks:

User → ManyToMany → Group table

That’s why this method is professional and clean.

⚠ If Dashboard Shows "No Role Assigned"
It means:

Group not created
OR

User not added to group

Double check both.

Tell me:

Did Student login show Student Dashboard?


Uploaded image
Uploaded image

Perfect ✅🔥

✅ STEP 3: Update Dashboard Logic (IMPORTANT FIX)
Your current dashboard checks request.user.role
But we are NOT using a role field in User model ❌

We are using Django Groups instead ✔

So update your core/views.py dashboard like this:

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from django.http import HttpResponse

@login_required
def dashboard(request):

    if request.user.is_superuser:
        return HttpResponse("Admin Dashboard")

    elif request.user.groups.filter(name='students').exists():
        return HttpResponse("Student Dashboard")

    elif request.user.groups.filter(name='faculty').exists():
        return HttpResponse("Faculty Dashboard")

    else:
        return HttpResponse("Logged in, but no group assigned.")
🔹 Why This Works
Instead of:

request.user.role
We now check:

request.user.groups.filter(name='students').exists()
That means:
👉 If user belongs to students group → show student dashboard
👉 If faculty group → show faculty dashboard

Professional method ✅

✅ STEP 4: Test Everything
Now:

Logout from admin

Go to:
http://127.0.0.1:8000/accounts/login/

🔹 student1
You should see:

Student Dashboard
🔹 faculty1
You should see:

Faculty Dashboard
🔹 Superuser
You should see:

Admin Dashboard

Very good 🔥🔥
Server is running perfectly ✅
No system errors ✅
Groups created ✅

But I found your real issue 👇

❌ Problem
Not Found: /admin/auth/user/
404
That means:

👉 The User model is not registered in admin
OR
👉 You removed default auth URLs accidentally
OR
👉 You changed admin configuration

✅ Let’s Fix It Properly
🔹 Step 1: Check config/urls.py
Open:

college_erp/config/urls.py
Make sure it looks like this:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('core.urls')),
]
⚠ VERY IMPORTANT:
You MUST have:

path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
If missing → add it.

🔹 Step 2: Check If auth is installed
Open:

config/settings.py
Make sure inside INSTALLED_APPS you have:

'django.contrib.admin',
'django.contrib.auth',
'django.contrib.contenttypes',
'django.contrib.sessions',
'django.contrib.messages',
'django.contrib.staticfiles',
If django.contrib.auth is missing → ADD IT.

🔹 Step 3: Try Correct URL
Instead of:

/admin/auth/user/
Try:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/auth/user/
⚠ Make sure:

You are logged in as superuser

URL is typed correctly (user NOT users)

🔥 IMPORTANT
Your logs show:

GET /admin/auth/ 200
GET /admin/auth/group/ 200
So auth is working ✔
Group is working ✔

But user page not found ❌

That usually means:

👉 You changed AUTH_USER_MODEL
OR
👉 You created custom user model
OR
👉 You removed default User from admin

🔎 Quick Check
Open:

core/admin.py
If you see something like:

from django.contrib.auth.models import User
Make sure you did NOT unregister it.

🛠 Fastest Fix
In core/admin.py, add this:

from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin
from django.contrib import admin

admin.site.register(User, UserAdmin)
Save → server auto reload → try again.

Check settings.py

Add User registration in admin.py

Restart server (Ctrl + C → python manage.py runserver)

Perfect 🔥🔥 You are VERY close now.

Your login worked ✅
But after login you got:

Not Found: /dashboard/
404 error
That means:

👉 Login successful
👉 But Django cannot find URL for /dashboard/

So now we fix this in 2 minutes.

🎯 WHY This Happened
After login, Django redirects to:

/dashboard/
But in your main config/urls.py, maybe dashboard URL is not connected properly.

✅ STEP 1 — Open config/urls.py
Open:

college_erp/config/urls.py
Make sure it looks like this:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('accounts/', include('accounts.urls')),
    path('', include('core.urls')),   # VERY IMPORTANT
]
⚠️ That last line is important.

If instead you wrote:

path('dashboard/', include('core.urls')),
Then /dashboard/ won't work correctly.

✅ STEP 2 — Open core/urls.py
Open:

core/urls.py
Make sure it contains:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('dashboard/', views.dashboard, name='dashboard'),
    path('students/', views.student_list, name='student_list'),
]
✅ STEP 3 — Check LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL
Open:

config/settings.py
Make sure you have:

LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/dashboard/'
LOGOUT_REDIRECT_URL = '/accounts/login/'
🚀 After Fixing
Save all files
Server will auto-restart

Now go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/accounts/login/
Login again as student1 or faculty1.

It should now show:

Student Dashboard
OR

Faculty Dashboard
OR

Admin Dashboard

🧠 What Just Happened (Important Learning)
Login was working ✔
Redirect was working ✔
URL routing was broken ❌

Now we fix routing = system complete ✔

config/urls.py

core/urls.py

Let me verify once like a real mentor 👨‍💻🔥


from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path("", views.dashboard, name="home"),
    path('dashboard/', views.dashboard, name='dashboard'),

    path("students/", views.student_list, name="student_list"),
    path("students/add/", views.add_student, name="add_student"),
    path("students/edit/<int:pk>/", views.edit_student, name="edit_student"),
    path("students/delete/<int:pk>/", views.delete_student, name="delete_student"),
]    it is core/urls.py                                                                                                            """
URL configuration for config project.

The urlpatterns list routes URLs to views. For more information please see:
    https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/6.0/topics/http/urls/
Examples:
Function views
    1. Add an import:  from my_app import views
    2. Add a URL to urlpatterns:  path('', views.home, name='home')
Class-based views
    1. Add an import:  from other_app.views import Home
    2. Add a URL to urlpatterns:  path('', Home.as_view(), name='home')
Including another URLconf
    1. Import the include() function: from django.urls import include, path
    2. Add a URL to urlpatterns:  path('blog/', include('blog.urls'))
"""
from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),
    path('', include('core.urls')),   # ← this connects core app
] it is config.urls.py 







🔥 PERFECT. Your URLs are 100% correct.

So the 404 is NOT from URL routing.

That means only one thing:

👉 Your dashboard view is redirecting somewhere wrong
OR
👉 You were accessing /dashboard without trailing slash.

✅ First Try This
Open in browser exactly:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/dashboard/
⚠️ With the / at the end.

If that works → problem solved.

✅ If Still 404 — Check Your dashboard View
Open:

core/views.py
Show me your dashboard function.

It should look something like this:

from django.shortcuts import render
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

@login_required
def dashboard(request):
    if request.user.groups.filter(name='students').exists():
        return render(request, 'core/student_dashboard.html')

    elif request.user.groups.filter(name='faculty').exists():
        return render(request, 'core/faculty_dashboard.html')

    else:
        return render(request, 'core/admin_dashboard.html')
🔥 VERY IMPORTANT CHECK
Do these template files exist?

Inside:

core/templates/core/
You must have:

student_dashboard.html
faculty_dashboard.html
admin_dashboard.html
If any one is missing → Django throws error.

🎯 Also Confirm settings.py
Make sure you have:

LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/dashboard/'
NOT:

LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = 'dashboard'
It must have slashes.

🧠 Why This Is Happening
Your routing:

'' → core.urls
'dashboard/' → views.dashboard
So /dashboard/ absolutely exists.

If 404 still appears, 99% chance:

Wrong redirect URL

Missing trailing slash

Browser cached old redirect

Try hard refresh:

Ctrl + Shift + R
🚀 Do This Now
Manually open /dashboard/

Hard refresh

Paste your dashboard view here

We’ll finish this cleanly 💪🔥

This error is VERY common and very easy to fix ✅
Nothing is wrong with your project.

❌ Problem
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'
👉 Your virtual environment is not activated
So Python cannot find Django.

✅ FIX (Most Likely Solution)
You created a virtual environment earlier (probably named venv).

From inside:

C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp
Run:

venv\Scripts\activate
After activating, you should see something like:

(venv) PS C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp>
Then run:

python manage.py runserver
🟢 If You Forgot to Create Virtual Environment
If activation fails, create it again:

python -m venv venv
Then activate:

venv\Scripts\activate
Then install Django:

pip install django
Then run server:

python manage.py runserver
🧠 Why This Happened
When you restart:

VS Code

Terminal

Computer

Virtual environment turns OFF automatically.

You must activate it every time before running the project.

🎯 Pro Tip (Very Important Habit)
Every time you open your project:

cd college_erp
venv\Scripts\activate
python manage.py runserver
Always this order.

Most common reasons:

❌ Wrong password

❌ User is not active

❌ Password not set properly

❌ User not assigned to group properly

If password not set correctly

Or browser auto-fill wrong password

Or typo

Login fails but page reloads (200).

🎯 Important Rule
In Django logs:

Status	Meaning
200	Page loaded / login failed
302	Redirect / login success
404	Page not found

Perfect 👍 you are doing real developer work now.

Let’s clearly understand what is happening.

🔴 Current Situation
This line:

POST /admin/login/?next=/admin/  → 200
Means:

👉 Admin login is still failing
👉 If login was successful → it would show 302

Even after password change.

So now this is NOT password issue.

This is a Custom User Model admin configuration issue.

You are using:

AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.CustomUser'
So Django default admin login system must properly recognize your CustomUser.

🎯 Step 1: Check Superuser Properly
Run this:

python manage.py shell
Then:

from accounts.models import CustomUser
CustomUser.objects.filter(is_superuser=True)
If it returns empty queryset → ❌ no superuser exists.

If it returns your user → good.

🎯 Step 2: SAFEST FIX (Create Fresh Superuser)
Instead of changing password, do this:

Stop server.

Then run:

python manage.py createsuperuser
Now enter:

Username: admin
Email: (anything)
Password: Strong one (like Admin@12345)

It should say:

Superuser created successfully.
🚀 Then Start Server
python manage.py runserver
Go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
Login with:

Username: admin
Password: Admin@12345

Now watch terminal:

If success → you MUST see:

POST /admin/login/  → 302
⚠️ Important Question
Did you register CustomUser in admin properly?

Open:

accounts/admin.py
It MUST contain something like:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin
from .models import CustomUser

admin.site.register(CustomUser, UserAdmin)
If this is missing → admin login breaks.

🧠 Also Important
You ran this many times:

django-admin startproject college_erp
Inside same folder.

That can create nested project structure and break settings.

We must confirm structure:

In your main folder, run:

dir
You should see:

manage.py
college_erp/
accounts/
core/
students/
...
NOT:

college_erp/
    college_erp/
        manage.py
If nested → we clean it.

🧠 Why This Is Happening
When using CustomUser:

If created before setting AUTH_USER_MODEL → DB mismatch

If superuser created before migration → mismatch

If admin not registered properly → login fails silently

🎯 Now Do This
Run createsuperuser again (fresh user)

Confirm CustomUser registered in admin.py

Tell me:

Does POST show 302 now?

We will fix this completely.

You are building a real ERP now 🔥


(venv) PS C:\Users\lenovo\college_erp> python manage.py shell

Very good 🔥
This output is IMPORTANT.

<QuerySet [<CustomUser: prateek>]>
✅ Superuser exists
❌ But admin login still failing

So now the issue is NOT:

Superuser ❌

Password (most likely ❌)

Database ❌

Now 90% chance issue is:

🔴 CustomUser Model Configuration Problem
🎯 Step 1 — Show Me Your CustomUser Model
Open:

accounts/models.py
And check:

Does it look like this?

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser
from django.db import models

class CustomUser(AbstractUser):
    pass
OR did you modify username field?

🚨 VERY IMPORTANT CHECK
Inside your CustomUser model:

Is this present?
USERNAME_FIELD = 'email'
If YES → then problem found ✅

Because Django admin login uses USERNAME_FIELD.

If you changed it to email but while logging in you are typing username → login fails.

🎯 Step 2 — Check This In Shell
In shell run:

user = CustomUser.objects.get(username="prateek")
user.check_password("your_password_here")
If it returns:

True
Password correct.

If False → password wrong.

🎯 Step 3 — Check is_active
In shell run:

user.is_active
If False → login blocked.

If True → OK.

🎯 Step 4 — Check Admin Registration
Open:

accounts/admin.py
It MUST be:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin
from .models import CustomUser

admin.site.register(CustomUser, UserAdmin)
If you just did:

admin.site.register(CustomUser)
Sometimes permissions issue happens.

🎯 Most Common Real Reason (I have seen this many times)
If you created superuser BEFORE setting:

AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.CustomUser'
Then DB tables mismatch silently.

💥 Ultimate Clean Fix (Guaranteed)
If nothing works:

1️⃣ Delete database file (db.sqlite3)
2️⃣ Delete migrations inside all apps (except init.py)
3️⃣ Run:

python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
python manage.py createsuperuser
python manage.py runserver
This will 100% fix admin login.

🧠 Before Resetting — Answer Me:
In your CustomUser model:

Did you modify USERNAME_FIELD?

Did you remove username field?

Did you make email unique?

Send me your models.py code.

We will solve this properly like real backend engineers 💻🔥

If admin login shows:

POST /admin/login/ → 200
Means:
❌ Login failed

If shows:

POST /admin/login/ → 302
Means:
✅ Login success

✅ STEP 1 — Add Role Field in CustomUser
Open:

accounts/models.py
Update your model like this:

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser
from django.db import models

class CustomUser(AbstractUser):

    ROLE_CHOICES = (
        ('ADMIN', 'Admin'),
        ('FACULTY', 'Faculty'),
        ('STUDENT', 'Student'),
    )

    role = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=ROLE_CHOICES)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.username
✅ STEP 2 — Make Migrations
Run:

python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate
✅ STEP 3 — Update Admin Panel
Open:

accounts/admin.py
Update like this:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin
from .models import CustomUser

class CustomUserAdmin(UserAdmin):
    fieldsets = UserAdmin.fieldsets + (
        ('Role Information', {'fields': ('role',)}),
    )

admin.site.register(CustomUser, CustomUserAdmin)
Restart server.

Now in admin → Custom Users → you will see Role field 🎉

✅ STEP 4 — Assign Roles
Go to Admin panel:

Set prateek1 → ADMIN

Create new user → faculty1 → FACULTY

Create new user → student1 → STUDENT

✅ STEP 5 — Create Dashboards
We create 3 dashboards.

Open:

core/views.py
Add:

from django.shortcuts import render
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

@login_required
def admin_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "core/admin_dashboard.html")

@login_required
def faculty_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "core/faculty_dashboard.html")

@login_required
def student_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "core/student_dashboard.html")
✅ STEP 6 — Role-Based Redirect After Login
Open:

accounts/views.py
Add:

from django.shortcuts import redirect
from django.contrib.auth import login
from django.contrib.auth.forms import AuthenticationForm

def login_view(request):
    form = AuthenticationForm(request, data=request.POST or None)

    if form.is_valid():
        user = form.get_user()
        login(request, user)

        if user.role == 'ADMIN':
            return redirect('admin_dashboard')
        elif user.role == 'FACULTY':
            return redirect('faculty_dashboard')
        elif user.role == 'STUDENT':
            return redirect('student_dashboard')

    return render(request, 'accounts/login.html', {'form': form})
✅ STEP 7 — Add URLs
In:

core/urls.py
Add:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin-dashboard/', views.admin_dashboard, name='admin_dashboard'),
    path('faculty-dashboard/', views.faculty_dashboard, name='faculty_dashboard'),
    path('student-dashboard/', views.student_dashboard, name='student_dashboard'),
]
Include in main urls.py:

path('', include('core.urls')),

>> python manage.py migrate
It is impossible to add a non-nullable field 'role' to customuser without specifying a default. This is because the database needs something to populate existing rows.
Please select a fix:
 1) Provide a one-off default now (will be set on all existing rows with a null value for this column)
 2) Quit and manually define a default value in models.py.
Select an option:

🔥 Very good — this is a REAL developer moment.

Django is telling you:

You already have users in database (prateek, prateek1)
Now you are adding a new required field role
But existing rows don’t have any value for it

So database is confused 🤯

🎯 What Should You Choose?
Select:

1
👉 Press 1 and hit Enter.

Then Django will ask:

Please enter the default value as valid Python:
Now type:

'ADMIN'
(with quotes)

Then press Enter.

✅ What This Does
It will:

✔ Set role = 'ADMIN' for all existing users
✔ Apply migration successfully
✔ Keep your data safe

🚀 After Migration
Run:

python manage.py migrate
If not already completed.

Then run:

python manage.py runserver
🧠 Why This Happened
Because you defined:

role = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=ROLE_CHOICES)
Without:

null=True

default=

blank=True

So Django treats it as REQUIRED field.

🧠 Professional Way (Better Approach)
After migration, improve your model like this:

role = models.CharField(
    max_length=20,
    choices=ROLE_CHOICES,
    default='STUDENT'
)
So future users automatically get STUDENT role.

STEP 1 – Create Login View
Open:

accounts/views.py
Add this:

from django.shortcuts import render, redirect
from django.contrib.auth import authenticate, login
from django.contrib import messages

def login_view(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        username = request.POST.get("username")
        password = request.POST.get("password")

        user = authenticate(request, username=username, password=password)

        if user is not None:
            login(request, user)

            if user.role == "ADMIN":
                return redirect("admin_dashboard")
            elif user.role == "FACULTY":
                return redirect("faculty_dashboard")
            elif user.role == "STUDENT":
                return redirect("student_dashboard")
        else:
            messages.error(request, "Invalid username or password")

    return render(request, "accounts/login.html")
STEP 2 – Create Dashboards (Temporary Simple Ones)
In same file:

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

@login_required
def admin_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/admin_dashboard.html")

@login_required
def faculty_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/faculty_dashboard.html")

@login_required
def student_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/student_dashboard.html")
STEP 3 – Add URLs
Open:

accounts/urls.py
Add:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('login/', views.login_view, name='login'),
    path('admin-dashboard/', views.admin_dashboard, name='admin_dashboard'),
    path('faculty-dashboard/', views.faculty_dashboard, name='faculty_dashboard'),
    path('student-dashboard/', views.student_dashboard, name='student_dashboard'),
]
STEP 4 – Include in Main urls.py
Open:

config/urls.py
Add:

path('', include('accounts.urls')),
(If not already added)

✅ FIX (Very Important Step)
Open:

📂 accounts/admin.py
Replace everything inside with this:

from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin
from .models import CustomUser


class CustomUserAdmin(UserAdmin):

    model = CustomUser

    fieldsets = UserAdmin.fieldsets + (
        ('Role Information', {'fields': ('role',)}),
    )

    add_fieldsets = UserAdmin.add_fieldsets + (
        ('Role Information', {'fields': ('role',)}),
    )


admin.site.register(CustomUser, CustomUserAdmin)

🚀 NOW — Let’s Build Role-Based Login System
This is where your ERP becomes next-level.

We will make:

Role	After Login Goes To
ADMIN	Admin Dashboard
FACULTY	Faculty Dashboard
STUDENT	Student Dashboard
🔥 STEP 1 — Create Login View
Open:

📂 accounts/views.py
Add this:

from django.shortcuts import render, redirect
from django.contrib.auth import authenticate, login
from django.contrib import messages
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

def login_view(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        username = request.POST.get("username")
        password = request.POST.get("password")

        user = authenticate(request, username=username, password=password)

        if user is not None:
            login(request, user)

            if user.role == "ADMIN":
                return redirect("admin_dashboard")
            elif user.role == "FACULTY":
                return redirect("faculty_dashboard")
            elif user.role == "STUDENT":
                return redirect("student_dashboard")
        else:
            messages.error(request, "Invalid Username or Password")

    return render(request, "accounts/login.html")


@login_required
def admin_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/admin_dashboard.html")


@login_required
def faculty_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/faculty_dashboard.html")


@login_required
def student_dashboard(request):
    return render(request, "accounts/student_dashboard.html")
🔥 STEP 2 — Create URLs
Open:

📂 accounts/urls.py
If not created, create it.

Add:

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('login/', views.login_view, name='login'),
    path('admin-dashboard/', views.admin_dashboard, name='admin_dashboard'),
    path('faculty-dashboard/', views.faculty_dashboard, name='faculty_dashboard'),
    path('student-dashboard/', views.student_dashboard, name='student_dashboard'),
]
🔥 STEP 3 — Connect URLs to Project
Open:

📂 config/urls.py
Add:

from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('accounts.urls')),
]

✅ STEP 2 — ROLE-BASED PAGE PROTECTION 🔒
Right now any logged-in user can manually open:

/admin-dashboard/
/faculty-dashboard/
/student-dashboard/
We must block wrong roles.

📂 accounts/views.py
Update dashboard views like this:

@login_required
def admin_dashboard(request):
    if request.user.role != "ADMIN":
        return redirect("login")
    return render(request, "accounts/admin_dashboard.html")


@login_required
def faculty_dashboard(request):
    if request.user.role != "FACULTY":
        return redirect("login")
    return render(request, "accounts/faculty_dashboard.html")


@login_required
def student_dashboard(request):
    if request.user.role != "STUDENT":
        return redirect("login")
    return render(request, "accounts/student_dashboard.html")

    <title>ERP Login</title>
    <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
</head>
<body class="bg-dark d-flex justify-content-center align-items-center vh-100">

<div class="card p-4 shadow" style="width: 350px;">
    <h3 class="text-center mb-3">College ERP Login</h3>

    <form method="POST">
        {% csrf_token %}
        <input type="text" name="username" class="form-control mb-3" placeholder="Username">
        <input type="password" name="password" class="form-control mb-3" placeholder="Password">
        <button type="submit" class="btn btn-primary w-100">Login</button>
    </form>

    {% for message in messages %}
        <p class="text-danger mt-2">{{ message }}</p>
    {% endfor %}
</div>

</body>
</html>
2️⃣ Professional Admin Dashboard
Replace admin_dashboard.html with:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Admin Dashboard</title>
    <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
</head>
<body class="bg-light">

<nav class="navbar navbar-dark bg-dark px-4">
    <span class="navbar-brand">Admin Panel</span>
    <a href="{% url 'logout' %}" class="btn btn-danger btn-sm">Logout</a>
</nav>

<div class="container mt-4">
    <h2>Welcome, {{ request.user.username }}</h2>
    <p>Role: {{ request.user.role }}</p>

    <div class="row mt-4">
        <div class="col-md-4">
            <div class="card p-3 shadow">
                <h5>Total Students</h5>
                <p>Manage Students Module</p>
            </div>
        </div>
        <div class="col-md-4">
            <div class="card p-3 shadow">
                <h5>Faculty Management</h5>
                <p>Manage Faculty Module</p>
            </div>
        </div>
    </div>
</div>

</body>
</html>
You can copy same layout style for:

faculty_dashboard.html

student_dashboard.html

Just change title and heading.

🚀 STEP 1 — BASE TEMPLATE SYSTEM (Professional Structure)
Ab hum ek master layout banayenge jisse sab dashboards inherit karenge.

📂 Create File
Location:

accounts/templates/accounts/base.html
🧱 Paste This Code in base.html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>College ERP</title>
    <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
</head>
<body>

<!-- Navbar -->
<nav class="navbar navbar-dark bg-dark px-3">
    <span class="navbar-brand">College ERP System</span>
    <div>
        <span class="text-white me-3">{{ request.user.username }}</span>
        <a href="{% url 'logout' %}" class="btn btn-danger btn-sm">Logout</a>
    </div>
</nav>

<div class="container-fluid">
    <div class="row">
        
        <!-- Sidebar -->
        <div class="col-md-2 bg-light vh-100 p-3">
            <h5>Menu</h5>
            <ul class="nav flex-column">
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Dashboard</a></li>
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Students</a></li>
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Faculty</a></li>
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Courses</a></li>
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Attendance</a></li>
                <li class="nav-item"><a class="nav-link" href="#">Exams</a></li>
            </ul>
        </div>

        <!-- Main Content -->
        <div class="col-md-10 p-4">
            {% block content %}
            {% endblock %}
        </div>

    </div>
</div>

</body>
</html>
🚀 STEP 2 — Update Dashboards (EXTEND BASE)
Ab open:

admin_dashboard.html
Replace everything with:

{% extends 'accounts/base.html' %}

{% block content %}
<h2>Admin Dashboard</h2>
<hr>

<div class="row">
    <div class="col-md-4">
        <div class="card shadow p-3">
            <h5>Total Students</h5>
            <h3>{{ total_students }}</h3>
        </div>
    </div>

    <div class="col-md-4">
        <div class="card shadow p-3">
            <h5>Total Faculty</h5>
            <h3>{{ total_faculty }}</h3>
        </div>
    </div>
</div>
{% endblock %}
Do same for:

faculty_dashboard.html
{% extends 'accounts/base.html' %}

{% block content %}
<h2>Faculty Dashboard</h2>
<hr>
<p>Welcome Faculty Member</p>
{% endblock %}
student_dashboard.html
{% extends 'accounts/base.html' %}

{% block content %}
<h2>Student Dashboard</h2>
<hr>
<p>Welcome Student</p>
{% endblock %}
🚀 STEP 3 — Dynamic Data (Option 2)
Open:

📂 accounts/views.py

Inside admin_dashboard view update:

from django.contrib.auth import get_user_model

User = get_user_model()

def admin_dashboard(request):
    total_students = User.objects.filter(role='student').count()
    total_faculty = User.objects.filter(role='faculty').count()

    context = {
        'total_students': total_students,
        'total_faculty': total_faculty,
    }

    return render(request, 'accounts/admin_dashboard.html', context)
🚀 STEP 4 — Role Security Double Check
Make sure admin_dashboard has:

if request.user.role != 'admin':
    return redirect('login')
Same logic for faculty & student dashboards.

✅ Short Answer:
🔥 Haan — Step 2 me jo dashboards diye maine
👉 Unko override (replace) karna hai
pehle wale simple dashboard files ke upar.

📌 Exactly Kya Replace Hoga?
Tumhare paas abhi probably simple version hoga:

<h2>Welcome Admin</h2>
<a href="{% url 'logout' %}">Logout</a>
Ab isko poora hata dena hai
Aur jo maine {% extends 'accounts/base.html' %} wala code diya hai
👉 usse replace kar dena hai.

📂 Files Jisme Replace Karna Hai
Inside:

accounts/templates/accounts/
Replace these 3 files completely:

✅ admin_dashboard.html

✅ faculty_dashboard.html

✅ student_dashboard.html

❗ Important Rule
Agar file me already content hai:

👉 Delete everything
👉 Paste new code
👉 Save

🚀 Base.html Naya Hai
Ye file nayi create hogi:

accounts/templates/accounts/base.html
Isko kisi ko override nahi karna — ye new file hai.

🧠 Step 3 (Dynamic Data)
accounts/views.py me:

👉 Sirf admin_dashboard function ko update karna hai
poori file delete nahi karni.

Bas function ko modify karo.

🎯 Final Structure Clear Kar Lo
After all updates:

accounts/
│
├── views.py  (updated)
│
└── templates/
    └── accounts/
        ├── base.html  (new)
        ├── admin_dashboard.html (replaced)
        ├── faculty_dashboard.html (replaced)
        └── student_dashboard.html (replaced)

Override karna galat nahi hota.

Version control (Git) me real developers regularly override & refactor karte hain.

Tum growth mode me ho — safe zone me nahi 😎🔥

SHABAASH PRATEEK 🔥💻

Ab next 3 cheezein check karni hain. Step by step. Panic nahi.

✅ STEP 1 — Server Run Karo
Terminal me:

python manage.py runserver
Phir browser me:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/
Login as admin.

✅ STEP 2 — Check UI
Admin dashboard me ye dekhna hai:

✔ Navbar top pe
✔ Sidebar left side
✔ Username show ho raha
✔ Logout button working
✔ Total Students number aa raha
✔ Total Faculty number aa raha

❗ Agar Count Show Nahi Ho Raha
To iska matlab hai:

views.py update nahi hua hai.

Open:

📂 accounts/views.py

Make sure admin_dashboard function aisa ho:

from django.contrib.auth import get_user_model
from django.shortcuts import render, redirect

User = get_user_model()

def admin_dashboard(request):
    if request.user.role != 'admin':
        return redirect('login')

    total_students = User.objects.filter(role='student').count()
    total_faculty = User.objects.filter(role='faculty').count()

    context = {
        'total_students': total_students,
        'total_faculty': total_faculty,
    }

    return render(request, 'accounts/admin_dashboard.html', context)
Save karo.
Server automatically reload karega.

🔥 Base template system
🔥 Sidebar layout
